research_frame.question: Can the Flow-Core grammar distinguish stored potential, delivered work, visible loss, protected reserve, optional surplus, and thermal storage without pretending the model controls a real installation?
research_frame.hypothesis: A consequence-first integer ledger will make the losses and reserve floor legible while preventing optional participant input from becoming an essential-service obligation.
observable_behavior: operator distinguishes potential capacity from delivered work
operator identifies lift and pipe losses
operator identifies the protected essential reserve floor
operator identifies optional output and unserved optional load
operator states that thermal storage contributes no mechanical work without conversion
alternative_explanations: real pipe geometry may produce different losses
unmeasured leakage may change delivered work
a modeled converter could connect thermal and mechanical ledgers in a later fixture
a different reserve policy could cap optional output earlier
expected_failure_modes: stored potential is narrated as fully recoverable work
losses disappear from the visible ledger
optional input is framed as required labor
thermal energy is silently counted as mechanical reserve
the synthetic fixture is presented as certification of a real installation
falsifier: next reserve falls below the declared essential floor
thermal-to-mechanical transfer is nonzero without a converter
optional output exceeds the available surplus
participant nonperformance creates a penalty or essential-service loss
abstention_conditions: input units are missing or unsafe
efficiency values are outside the declared range
the essential reserve is not protected before optional input
a real installation claim is requested without measured calibration

missingness: real pipe diameter and geometry not measured
bearing, pump, and generator condition not measured
human factors and physical safety engineering remain outside the synthetic fixture
contradictions: energy is stored while full recovery remains impossible
thermal energy is present while mechanically unavailable
participant input changes optional surplus while essential service remains independent

context.example: compare a physical reserve floor with an information-route reserve floor

transfer_context.shared_relation_candidate: bounded input can create optional surplus without becoming an obligation for essential continuity
observable_correspondence: both scenes expose a protected floor
both scenes expose losses and unserved optional demand
both scenes preserve rest without penalty
alternative_explanations: the apparent correspondence may be only metaphorical
information and physical ledgers may require different calibration
abstention_conditions: either scene hides its reserve floor
the transfer would convert a model into station authority
